title: Make the Information Structure Survive without CSS
summary: Headings label sections and express an outline; paragraphs carry prose; lists express collections or sequences; block quotations identify quoted passages; and code elements identify computer input or output. Element choice should preserve meaning when presentation is removed. Predict, inspect, explain, and apply the contract in a cumulative public-information build.
objectives: Explain html text headings lists with source and browser evidence.
Apply html text headings lists without discarding earlier document work.
Diagnose the named misconception in a changed public-service scenario.

steps.why: Make the Information Structure Survive without CSS requires this decision because the learner must distinguish a specification-defined relationship from a browser-recovered appearance. This is checkpoint 1 in the public health copy desk.

options.text: Public health copy desk case 1: a sequence of br elements communicates the same list relationship as ol or ul.
Public health copy desk case 1: select text elements for the relationship they communicate, then use CSS later for visual scale.
Public health copy desk case 1: every sentence should be a heading because headings receive stronger default styling.

public health copy desk phase 2.2: group related content beneath section headings
public health copy desk phase 2.3: represent collections and procedures with the correct list type
public health copy desk phase 2.4: read the outline without presentation to verify the relationships
